recapTopic: AI Visibility and Generative Engine Optimization for B2B

recap.intro: Hey everyone! This week, we dove into how businesses can get noticed by AI, especially when people are using tools like ChatGPT to find information. It's all about making sure your company pops up when AI is looking for answers!
bullets: First, we saw that getting your business found by AI, which we call Generative Engine Optimization (GEO), is a lot like how regular search engine optimization (SEO) started years ago – it's a new frontier we're just beginning to explore.

Building on that, we learned just how powerful getting recommended by AI can be for your business. For example, brands that AI recommended actually saw 2.5 times more website visits, showing a huge boost in traffic!

Finally, the big picture is that when you ask ChatGPT something, it's secretly 'Googling' things in the background, finding web pages, and then using those to give you an answer. This means your business needs to show up high in those hidden searches to get mentioned by the AI. We found out these secret searches are super different from what people usually type – they're long, conversational, and even consider personal details like if someone is vegan, which makes predicting them tricky. A wild example showed us that Reddit's mentions in ChatGPT answers dropped from 15% to under 2% almost overnight when Google changed a technical setting, proving AI's answers are totally linked to regular search results. Luckily, tools like QueryFan can help you figure out these secret AI searches by acting like different people asking questions.

recapResources.headline: GEO is following the same path as early SEO
AI-Recommended Brands Saw 2.5x More Site Visits: Similarweb via @sejournal, @MattGSouthern
ChatGPT Is Secretly Googling Things: This Tool Shows You Exactly What

topic: B2B Demand Generation and Pipeline Growth
headline: What the 2026 ABM Benchmark Survey Reveals About B2B Growth Strategy
keyNotes: Surprisingly, a 2026 survey shows that a focused marketing approach called Account-Based Marketing (ABM) is now a central way for companies that sell to other businesses (B2B organizations) to grow.
This means ABM is seen as a "core growth engine," indicating it's no longer a niche tactic but a fundamental strategy for business expansion.

Almost half (47%) of these business-to-business companies are actively combining Account-Based Marketing (ABM) with "demand generation," which is the process of creating interest and desire for their products or services.
question: Since Account-Based Marketing (ABM) is becoming a core way for businesses to grow, how might your own company or industry benefit from focusing marketing efforts on specific, high-value customers, rather than trying to reach everyone?
